questions.stem: Which gas was essentially absent from the atmosphere of early Earth?
questions.explanation: Early Earth lacked free oxygen (and therefore ozone), while carbon dioxide and methane were more abundant.

questions.stem: Because early Earth had no ozone layer, its surface received high levels of:
questions.explanation: With no free oxygen there was no ozone, so more ultraviolet light penetrated to the surface.

questions.stem: The conditions on early Earth are thought to have allowed carbon compounds to form:

options.text: Only in the absence of any energy

questions.explanation: The conditions may have caused a variety of carbon compounds to form spontaneously by chemical processes that do not occur now.

questions.stem: Higher concentrations of carbon dioxide and methane on early Earth would have resulted in:
questions.explanation: The greenhouse gases carbon dioxide and methane raised temperatures on early Earth.

questions.stem: What is described as the smallest unit of self-sustaining life?
questions.explanation: Cells are the smallest units of self-sustaining life.

questions.stem: Why are viruses considered non-living?

options.text: They cannot sustain life processes independently of a host
They are made of only lipids

questions.explanation: Viruses are non-living because they cannot carry out self-sustaining life processes without a host cell.

questions.stem: How are all cells produced today?
questions.explanation: Cells are highly complex and can currently only be produced by the division of pre-existing cells.

questions.stem: Which of the following was NOT a necessary requirement for the evolution of the first cells?
questions.explanation: Catalysis, self-replication, self-assembly and compartmentalization were required; early Earth lacked free oxygen for aerobic respiration.

questions.scenario: A hypothesis about the origin of life cannot be tested by recreating the exact conditions of early Earth.
questions.stem: What does this illustrate about scientific claims?

options.text: Some hypotheses are difficult to test even though claims must be testable
Scientific claims never need to be testable

questions.explanation: Claims in science must be testable, but scientists sometimes struggle with hypotheses that are hard to test, such as the origin of cells.

questions.scenario: The Miller–Urey experiment passed sparks through a mixture of gases thought to resemble early Earth's atmosphere.
questions.stem: What did the experiment demonstrate?

options.text: Organic carbon compounds can form from simpler inorganic molecules
Cells can be created from scratch
Oxygen was abundant on early Earth

questions.explanation: The Miller–Urey experiment provided evidence that carbon compounds could form spontaneously under early-Earth-like conditions.

questions.stem: Fatty acids can spontaneously form which structure important to the origin of cells?
questions.explanation: Fatty acids can coalesce into spherical bilayers, forming vesicles that create membrane-bound compartments.

questions.stem: Why was the formation of a membrane-bound compartment important for the first cells?

options.text: It allowed internal chemistry to differ from the surroundings
It provided a source of oxygen
It removed the need for genetic material

questions.explanation: A membrane compartment let the chemistry inside become different from that outside, a key step towards cells.

questions.stem: RNA is proposed as the first genetic material because, unlike DNA, it can:

options.text: Be replicated AND act as a catalyst

questions.explanation: RNA can be replicated and has catalytic activity, so it may have acted as both genetic material and enzyme in the earliest cells.

questions.stem: Which present-day example supports the idea that RNA can act as a catalyst?

options.text: Ribozymes in the ribosome catalysing peptide bond formation

questions.explanation: Ribozymes in the ribosome still catalyse peptide bond formation, showing RNA's catalytic ability.

questions.stem: What does LUCA stand for?
questions.explanation: LUCA is the last universal common ancestor from which all present-day life is descended.

questions.stem: Which of these is evidence for a last universal common ancestor?

options.text: The universal genetic code and shared genes across organisms
Different genetic codes in each kingdom
The absence of any shared genes

questions.explanation: The universal genetic code and genes shared across all organisms are evidence for a common ancestor, LUCA.

questions.stem: Why might other early forms of life have become extinct even if they existed?

options.text: Competition from LUCA and its descendants
They had better adaptations than LUCA

questions.explanation: Other forms of life may have evolved but become extinct due to competition from LUCA and its descendants.

questions.stem: Studying the dates of the first cells and LUCA helps students appreciate:

options.text: The immense length of time over which life has evolved
That dates cannot be estimated at all

questions.explanation: Estimating these dates develops an appreciation of the immense timescale over which life has been evolving.

questions.stem: LUCA is thought to have evolved in the vicinity of:
questions.explanation: Evidence suggests LUCA evolved in the vicinity of seafloor hydrothermal vents.

questions.stem: Which is cited as evidence for the evolution of LUCA near hydrothermal vents?

options.text: Fossilized life from ancient seafloor vent precipitates and conserved gene sequences
The presence of ozone in ancient rocks

questions.explanation: Evidence includes fossilized life from ancient seafloor hydrothermal vent precipitates and conserved sequences from genomic analysis.

questions.stem: Why do the spontaneous chemical processes that formed early carbon compounds not occur on Earth now?

options.text: Atmospheric conditions, including oxygen levels, have changed
There is no longer any water

questions.explanation: Today's atmosphere (notably its free oxygen) differs from early Earth, so those spontaneous processes no longer occur.

questions.scenario: A scientist debates whether a newly found particle is living.
questions.stem: Which feature would best support classifying something as living?

options.text: It is a self-sustaining unit carrying out life processes

questions.explanation: A defining feature of life is being a self-sustaining unit; this is why cells are living but viruses are not.

questions.stem: Explaining the spontaneous origin of cells is challenging mainly because cells are:
questions.explanation: The great complexity of cells makes explaining their spontaneous origin difficult.

questions.stem: The 'RNA world' hypothesis suggests that in the earliest cells, RNA served as:

options.text: Both the genetic material and the catalysts

questions.explanation: RNA may have initially acted as both the genetic material and the enzymes of the earliest cells.

questions.stem: Vesicles form when fatty acids arrange themselves into bilayers because fatty acids have:
questions.explanation: The dual nature of fatty acids drives them to self-assemble into spherical bilayers in water, forming vesicles.

questions.scenario: A student is asked to 'evaluate' the Miller–Urey experiment.
questions.stem: A valid limitation of the experiment is that:

options.text: The exact composition of early Earth's atmosphere is uncertain
It produced no organic molecules at all

questions.explanation: A limitation is that the true early atmosphere is uncertain, so the simulated conditions may not match reality.

questions.stem: The sharing of many of the same genes across bacteria, archaea and eukaryotes suggests:

options.text: That genes were invented separately each time

questions.explanation: Shared genes across all domains are evidence of descent from a last universal common ancestor.

questions.stem: Which combination best describes early Earth's atmosphere?

options.text: No free oxygen, high carbon dioxide and methane
High free oxygen, low carbon dioxide

questions.explanation: Early Earth had no free oxygen (and no ozone) but higher levels of carbon dioxide and methane.

questions.stem: Which is a reason a virus is NOT a self-sustaining unit of life?

options.text: It cannot metabolise or reproduce without a host cell
It is made only of DNA

questions.explanation: Viruses depend entirely on host cells for their life functions, so they are not self-sustaining and are non-living.

questions.stem: Which process allows a molecule to make copies of itself, a requirement for the first cells?
questions.explanation: Self-replication of molecules was one of the necessary requirements for the evolution of the first cells.

questions.stem: The spontaneous coming-together of molecules into ordered structures without external direction is called:
questions.explanation: Self-assembly of molecules was among the requirements for the emergence of the first cells.

questions.stem: Compartmentalization created by a vesicle membrane is significant because it enables:

options.text: A separation between internal and external chemistry

questions.explanation: Compartmentalization allows the internal chemistry to become different from the external environment.

questions.stem: A key advantage of RNA over DNA as a first genetic material is that RNA:

options.text: Has catalytic activity of its own

questions.explanation: Unlike DNA, RNA has some catalytic activity, so it could act as both information store and enzyme.

questions.stem: Estimating when the first cells appeared relies on interpreting evidence such as:
questions.explanation: Approaches to dating the first cells and LUCA draw on fossil and genomic evidence.

questions.stem: Hydrothermal vents are considered a plausible site for the origin of life because they provide:

options.text: Energy and chemicals for early reactions

questions.explanation: Seafloor hydrothermal vents supply energy and chemicals that could support the reactions needed for early life.

questions.stem: Which of these carbon-containing gases contributed to greenhouse warming on early Earth?
questions.explanation: Higher concentrations of the carbon compounds methane and carbon dioxide raised early temperatures.

questions.stem: The idea that some early life forms evolved but then died out implies LUCA was:

options.text: The ancestor whose descendants outcompeted the others
The very first molecule on Earth

questions.explanation: LUCA is the common ancestor of surviving life; other lineages may have existed but became extinct through competition.

questions.stem: The ability of molecules to speed up chemical reactions, required for the first cells, is called:
questions.explanation: Catalysis (speeding up reactions) was one of the necessary requirements for the emergence of cells.

questions.stem: The Miller–Urey experiment is best described as evidence for the:

options.text: Spontaneous formation of organic carbon compounds
Existence of LUCA at hydrothermal vents

questions.explanation: Miller–Urey provided evidence that organic carbon compounds could form spontaneously from simple molecules.

questions.stem: Ribozymes are best described as:

options.text: RNA molecules that act as catalysts

questions.explanation: Ribozymes are catalytic RNA molecules, such as those in the ribosome that catalyse peptide bond formation.

questions.stem: Distinguishing living from non-living things is difficult partly because:

options.text: Some entities, like viruses, share only some features of life
There are no shared features at all

questions.explanation: Discussing living versus non-living includes cases like viruses that share some but not all features of life.

questions.stem: 'Conserved sequences' used as evidence for LUCA refers to:

options.text: Gene or protein sequences shared with little change across organisms
Sequences that change rapidly every generation
The order of minerals in vent rocks

questions.explanation: Conserved sequences are those retained with little change across organisms, pointing to a common ancestor.

questions.stem: The absence of an ozone layer on early Earth is directly linked to the absence of:
questions.explanation: Ozone is formed from oxygen, so with no free oxygen there was no ozone layer.

questions.stem: A vesicle is best described as:
questions.explanation: A vesicle is a small membrane-bound compartment, here formed spontaneously from fatty acid bilayers.

questions.stem: Life is estimated to have been evolving on Earth for a period best described as:
questions.explanation: The dating of the first cells and LUCA emphasises the immense timescale of billions of years.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a reason it is hard to test hypotheses about the first protocells?

options.text: The first protocells did not fossilize and conditions cannot be exactly replicated
We can directly observe them forming

questions.explanation: The exact pre-biotic conditions cannot be replicated and the first protocells did not fossilize, making testing difficult.

questions.stem: The universal genetic code is powerful evidence for common ancestry because it is:
questions.explanation: The near-universal genetic code shared across life is strong evidence for descent from a common ancestor.

questions.stem: Which energy source did the Miller–Urey experiment use to drive reactions?
questions.explanation: Miller–Urey used electrical sparks to simulate lightning, driving the formation of organic molecules.

questions.stem: Which best completes the statement: 'A cell is the smallest unit that can...'

options.text: carry out all the functions of life independently

questions.explanation: Cells are the smallest self-sustaining units of life, able to carry out life's functions independently.

questions.stem: Fossilized evidence of early life at hydrothermal vents is found in:
questions.explanation: Fossilized life has been found in ancient seafloor hydrothermal vent precipitates, supporting a vent origin for LUCA.
